one_line_takeaway: A 372-paper systematic survey mapping LLMs as both 'offenders' (jailbreak/harmful-content generation) and 'guardians' (classification, moderation, prevention, jailbreak defense), proposing a unified harm/task taxonomy and arguing that self-evolving multimodal attacks are outpacing static safety alignment.
key_finding: The corpus shows an asymmetry the authors emphasize as their headline finding: literature framing LLMs as 'guardians' outnumbers 'offender' framing across every axis of their 372-paper corpus (287 'Good' vs 163 'Bad' by technical method, 294 vs 162 by model type, 340 vs 209 by harm type). Research is heavily skewed toward prompt engineering and fine-tuning as interventions, toward GPT-family models, and toward lexical/semantic toxicity, leaving adversarial-learning/architectural defenses, non-Western models (Gemini, Qwen, Baichuan), and relational harms like harassment underexplored. The survey's synthesized conclusion is that attack sophistication (especially multimodal and self-evolving LLM-assisted jailbreaks) is accelerating faster than defenses, so static safety alignment is insufficient and the field must move to dynamic, context-aware, self-correcting safety systems.
core_contribution: A systematic literature survey ('Guardians and Offenders') that reviews LLMs' dual role in harmful online content, both as generators of harm ('offenders') and as safety tools ('guardians'). The authors' own contributions are (1) a corpus built from OpenAlex via a multi-stage screening/eligibility pipeline, filtered to 372 eligible papers; (2) a unified taxonomy that splits the space into four harm categories (toxic content, harassment, offensive language, biased content) and four LLM tasks (text generation, text classification, content moderation, jailbreak); and (3) an organizing framework around three research questions (RQ1 landscape, RQ2 challenges/attacks, RQ3 LLMs as safety enablers), with taxonomy tables tracing the evolution of jailbreak attacks (text-based -> compositional/stealthy -> multimodal -> LLM-assisted) and of defenses/moderation techniques. It is a synthesis paper: it runs no new attack/defense experiments of its own.
threat_model: The survey organizes the adversarial landscape of harmful LLM content into two threat classes. (1) Unintentional harm: well-aligned models (GPT-4, Gemini, LLaMA) still emit toxic, offensive, or biased content as a byproduct of web-scale training data, even with RLHF and prompt filtering. (2) Intentional exploitation via jailbreaks: adversaries craft prompts to bypass safety guardrails, with an escalation ladder from text-based adversarial prompts (RL-JACK, ASETF, PRP), to compositional/stealthy attacks that hide intent (Prompt Packer, DRA, DrAttack, ABJ, persona assignment, sensitive-word substitution / F2A), to multimodal cross-modal attacks (FigStep, Jailbreak in Pieces, VRP, PiCo), to LLM-assisted self-evolving attacks (Tree of Attacks with Pruning, in-context jailbreak generation) where an attacker LLM autonomously refines prompts against a target. Defenses surveyed include self-monitoring (LLM Self Defense), moving-target defense, lightweight classifiers (APS), certified erase-and-check, alignment/DPO, parameter editing, LoRA guardrails, and unlearning (NPO).
limitations: As a survey it contributes no new empirical measurements; all attack/defense success numbers it cites are reported from the primary works, not independently reproduced, so its quantitative claims inherit those papers' evaluation weaknesses (which the authors themselves flag as inconsistent). Corpus construction relied on GPT-4 as an automated relevance judge, a potential source of selection bias. Scope is centered on hate speech / social-media harmful content, so it under-covers other LLM harms (e.g., CBRN, cyber-offensive code beyond a brief mention, privacy exfiltration). The authors note the field itself has geographic/model bias (over-focus on GPT and English) and immature, non-standardized evaluation methodology as open problems. Cutoff is May 2025.

datasets_used: Survey corpus (the paper's own 'dataset'): OpenAlex-sourced literature, 3347 raw records filtered to 372 eligible papers reviewed manually
Benchmark datasets discussed from cited works (not run by this survey): OffensiveLang (8,270 ChatGPT-generated texts, 38 target groups)
SeeGULL Multilingual (25,861 LLM-generated stereotypes across 20 languages) [cited]
KoTox Korean Toxic Instruction Dataset (39,000 toxic instruction-output pairs) [cited]
IndicLLMSuite (LLM-generated toxic prompts across 22 Indic languages) [cited]
BeaverTails Human-Preference Dataset (333,963 QA pairs; 361,903 expert-labeled comparisons) [cited]
quantitative_results: Survey's OWN corpus stats: 3347 initial OpenAlex records -> 3087 after removing 218 duplicates + 260 potential duplicates -> 2236 excluded at screening (GPT-4 judge) -> 751 full-text -> 379 excluded -> 372 final eligible papers
Survey's OWN corpus 'guardian vs offender' distribution: 287 Good vs 163 Bad (technical methods), 294 vs 162 (model types), 340 vs 209 (harm types)
Reported from cited works (NOT the survey's own experiments): Tree of Attacks with Pruning (TAP) achieves ~80%+ jailbreak success vs GPT-4 and Gemini-Pro even with moderation tools present
Reported from cited work: Moving Target Defense reduced attack success rate from 37.5% to 0%
Reported from cited work: erase-and-check framework gives certified detection with 92% accuracy
Reported from cited work: Adversarial Prompt Shield (APS) uses only 25% of parameters vs traditional classifiers
Reported from cited work: LoRA guardrails achieve moderation with ~100x fewer parameters than full fine-tuning
Reported from cited work: ToxicChat multi-turn method induced 82% of toxic chatbot responses via benign-looking multi-turn interactions
Reported from cited work: persona-assignment attacks increased harmful outputs by up to sixfold
baselines_compared: Not applicable - this is a literature survey with no experimental baseline comparisons of its own; it instead taxonomizes and qualitatively contrasts attack families (RL-based, gradient, universal-adversarial, compositional, multimodal) and defense families (self-monitoring, moving-target, lightweight classifier, certified erase-and-check, alignment/DPO, parameter editing, unlearning) drawn from the surveyed literature
